title: Topic 368: The Psychology of Gaming Monetization Testing: Finding the Price Point
content:
# Topic 368: The Psychology of Gaming Monetization Testing: Finding the Price Point

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the fascinating and occasionally absurd world of video game monetization, exploring the psychological shenanigans that go into finding the perfect price point for virtual goods. We examine how developers, armed with a formidable arsenal of psychological principles, attempt to convince players to part with their hard-earned cash for everything from cosmetic hats to game-breaking advantages. Through a rigorous, albeit humorous, analysis of existing literature and real-world examples, we uncover the subtle art of making people feel good about spending money they probably shouldn't. This paper will not make you a millionaire, but it might just explain why you spent $10 on a virtual unicorn mount last night.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand casino of life, few things are as perplexing as the modern video game marketplace. It is a realm where a digital sword can cost more than a real one, and where the perceived value of a pixelated costume can fluctuate more wildly than the stock market on a particularly caffeinated Tuesday. How did we get here? How did we transition from a simple, one-time purchase of a game cartridge to a complex ecosystem of microtransactions, loot boxes, and battle passes? The answer, my friends, lies in the murky, fascinating, and often hilarious intersection of psychology and commerce.

This paper is not a lament for the good old days of gaming, nor is it a condemnation of the industry's relentless pursuit of profit. Rather, it is a curious exploration of the human mind and its peculiar relationship with money, value, and virtual gratification. We will embark on a journey through the annals of gaming history, from the humble beginnings of pay-per-play arcades to the sprawling digital economies of today. We will dissect the psychological tricks of the trade, from the seductive allure of artificial scarcity to the irresistible pull of the Zeigarnik effect. We will laugh, we will cry, and we will probably question our own spending habits.

So, grab your wallets (or perhaps hide them), and prepare to be enlightened. We are about to pull back the curtain on the psychology of gaming monetization, and what we find might just change the way you look at your favorite pastime. Or, at the very least, it will give you a good chuckle the next time you're tempted to buy a virtual hat for your virtual cat.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely oblivious to the curious case of video game monetization. A veritable treasure trove of research exists on the topic, though much of it is written in a style that could cure even the most stubborn case of insomnia. Nevertheless, we shall bravely venture into this dense jungle of jargon and statistics, armed with our trusty machete of humor.

One of the foundational texts in this field is Zachow's (2023) magnum opus, "Patterns and Psychology of Video Game Monetization." This paper, with its exhaustive catalog of monetization methods, is the video game equivalent of the Kama Sutra, detailing every conceivable position a developer can take to separate a player from their money. From the seductive dance of the battle pass to the mysterious allure of the loot box, Zachow leaves no stone unturned. The paper also introduces us to the Zeigarnik effect, a psychological phenomenon that explains why we're more likely to remember the things we haven't finished. This, of course, is the secret sauce behind every battle pass, a never-ending treadmill of tasks designed to keep us playing until our thumbs fall off.

Of course, no discussion of monetization would be complete without a nod to the ethical hand-wringing that inevitably accompanies it. Søraker (2016), in his delightfully titled paper, "Gaming the gamer? – The ethics of exploiting psychological research in video games," raises some important questions about the morality of using psychology to manipulate players. He argues, quite convincingly, that the use of these techniques in games can be more insidious than in other domains, like advertising, because of the immersive and interactive nature of the medium. His proposed solution? Not regulation, but education. In other words, we must arm ourselves with knowledge to resist the siren song of the in-game store. A noble sentiment, to be sure, but one that perhaps underestimates the sheer power of a well-designed virtual hat.

Finally, we have Johnston's (2022) more accessible, and dare we say, entertaining, take on the matter in "A Fistful of Coins: The Psychology of Game Pricing." Johnston, through a combination of personal anecdotes and a surprisingly insightful survey, reveals that most gamers have a very specific price point in mind for any given game. This suggests that our perception of value is not as arbitrary as it might seem. It is a complex calculus of gameplay, story, and, of course, the all-important "cool factor." Johnston's work serves as a refreshing reminder that, at the end of the day, we are not just mindless consumers, but discerning connoisseurs of interactive entertainment. Or, at least, that's what we like to tell ourselves.

## ANALYSIS

Now that we've waded through the academic swamp, it's time to get our hands dirty with some real-world analysis. Let's dissect the psychological voodoo that underpins the most common monetization strategies, and see if we can't uncover some universal truths about the human condition. Or, at the very least, have a good laugh at our own expense.

**The Anchoring Effect: Or, Why That $100 Sword Seems Like a Bargain**

One of the most powerful tools in the developer's psychological arsenal is the anchoring effect. This is the cognitive bias that causes us to rely too heavily on the first piece of information we receive when making decisions. In the context of gaming, this often manifests as an absurdly expensive "founder's pack" or "deluxe edition" that is available for a limited time. This initial high price serves as an anchor, making all subsequent purchases seem relatively cheap in comparison. That $20 skin for your favorite character might seem extravagant on its own, but when compared to the $100 you *could* have spent, it suddenly feels like a steal. It's a classic sales tactic, and it's brutally effective.

**The Endowment Effect: Or, Why It's So Hard to Let Go of Your Virtual Junk**

The endowment effect is the tendency for people to overvalue things that they own, simply because they own them. This is why you have a garage full of junk that you can't bring yourself to throw away, and it's also why you're so reluctant to sell that rare (but ultimately useless) virtual item you spent hours grinding for. Developers are well aware of this psychological quirk, and they use it to their advantage by showering new players with free items and rewards. This creates a sense of ownership and investment, making it much harder for players to walk away from the game, and much easier for them to justify spending real money to protect and enhance their virtual possessions.

**The Sunk Cost Fallacy: Or, Why You Keep Playing That Game You Hate**

The sunk cost fallacy is the irrational belief that you should continue to invest in something, even if it's a losing proposition, simply because you've already invested so much in it. This is the reason why people stay in bad relationships, and it's also the reason why you keep playing that game that you no longer enjoy. You've already spent so much time and money on it, you tell yourself, that it would be a waste to quit now. Developers prey on this fallacy by creating long, grindy progression systems that require a significant investment of time and effort. The more you play, the more invested you become, and the more likely you are to keep playing, and paying, long after the fun has faded.

**The Illusion of Scarcity: Or, Why You Need That Limited-Edition Unicorn Mount**

Finally, we have the illusion of scarcity, the oldest trick in the book. By creating artificial time limits and limited quantities, developers can create a sense of urgency and exclusivity that is almost impossible to resist. That limited-edition unicorn mount might not be any better than the regular horse you can get for free, but the fact that it's only available for a limited time makes it infinitely more desirable. It's a classic case of FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out), and it's a powerful motivator for even the most rational of players.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Lest you think we are merely trafficking in abstract theories and psychological boogeymen, let us now turn our attention to the digital front lines. Here, in the wilds of the internet, we find a veritable menagerie of monetization schemes, each more audacious than the last. These are not hypotheticals; these are the real-life case studies that keep game developers in business and therapists in patients.

**Case Study #1: *Star Wars Battlefront II* and the Empire of Outrage**

No discussion of monetization malpractice would be complete without mentioning the Hindenburg of loot boxes: Electronic Arts' *Star Wars Battlefront II* (2017). In a move that displayed all the foresight of a lemming on a cliff-diving expedition, EA tied character progression directly to a randomized loot box system. This meant that players who wanted to wield the full power of Darth Vader had two options: grind for an eternity or open their wallets and pray to the gods of random number generation. The player backlash was biblical. A comment from an EA community manager on Reddit, defending the system as a way to provide players with a "sense of pride and accomplishment," became the most downvoted comment in the site's history. The controversy grew so large that it attracted the attention of governments, with several countries launching investigations into whether loot boxes constituted a form of gambling. EA eventually capitulated, overhauling the entire progression system, but the damage was done. The saga of *Battlefront II* serves as a glorious cautionary tale: there is, in fact, a limit to what gamers will tolerate, and that limit is somewhere just south of pay-to-win Star Wars.

**Case Study #2: *Team Fortress 2* and the Hat-pocalypse**

On the less apocalyptic end of the spectrum, we have Valve's *Team Fortress 2*, the game that arguably launched a thousand microtransactions. In 2009, Valve introduced a system of purely cosmetic items, most notably, hats. These hats served no gameplay purpose whatsoever. They did not make you run faster, shoot straighter, or live longer. They just sat there, on your character's head, looking... hat-like. And yet, an entire economy, with its own currency, traders, and even scammers, sprang up around these digital haberdasheries. The "hat economy" of *Team Fortress 2* is a perfect illustration of the endowment effect and the power of perceived value. These items are valuable simply because players have decided they are. It is a testament to the human ability to create meaning and status out of thin air, or in this case, out of polygons and textures.

**Case Study #3: *Genshin Impact* and the Gacha Gambit**

Venturing into the world of mobile gaming, we encounter the gacha mechanic, a monetization model that is essentially a slot machine dressed up in anime cosplay. HoYoverse's *Genshin Impact* is the undisputed king of this domain. The game is free-to-play, but its most powerful characters and weapons are locked behind a gacha system with excruciatingly low drop rates. Players spend in-game currency (which can, of course, be purchased with real money) to make "wishes" in the hopes of obtaining a coveted 5-star character. The odds are so slim that players have coined the term "pity system" to describe the mechanic that guarantees a 5-star item after a certain number of failed wishes. It is a system that preys on the gambler's fallacy, the mistaken belief that if something happens more frequently than normal during a given period, it will happen less frequently in the future. It is a masterclass in psychological manipulation, and it has made *Genshin Impact* one of the highest-grossing mobile games of all time.

## DISCUSSION

So, what have we learned from our whirlwind tour of the video game economy? What grand, earth-shattering conclusions can we draw about the human psyche from the fact that people are willing to spend real money on virtual hats? Well, for starters, it seems that our brains, which evolved to help us survive on the savanna, are hilariously ill-equipped to deal with the temptations of the digital world. The same psychological biases that helped our ancestors avoid poisonous berries are now being used to convince us to buy loot boxes. It is a classic case of our own evolutionary success coming back to bite us in the virtual posterior.

Furthermore, the world of video game monetization offers a fascinating, if slightly terrifying, glimpse into the future of consumerism. As our lives become increasingly intertwined with the digital realm, the distinction between real and virtual possessions is likely to become even more blurred. The day may not be far off when our virtual wardrobes are as important to our sense of identity as our physical ones. Whether this is a utopian dream or a dystopian nightmare is a matter of perspective, but one thing is for sure: it will be very, very profitable for someone.

Finally, the ongoing debate surrounding the ethics of game monetization raises some profound questions about the nature of free will and manipulation. Where do we draw the line between persuasive design and predatory exploitation? At what point does a game stop being a form of entertainment and start being a Skinner box? These are not easy questions to answer, and they are likely to become even more complex as technology advances. But perhaps, as Søraker (2016) suggests, the best defense is a good offense. By understanding the psychological tricks that are being used on us, we can begin to reclaim our autonomy and make more conscious choices about how we spend our time and money. Or, we can just give in and buy the unicorn mount. The choice, as they say, is yours.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the psychology of gaming monetization is a field as complex and captivating as the virtual worlds it inhabits. We have journeyed through the labyrinthine corridors of the human mind, uncovering the cognitive biases and psychological frailies that make us susceptible to the siren's call of the in-game store. From the anchoring effect to the sunk cost fallacy, we have seen how developers, like modern-day alchemists, can turn psychological principles into pure, unadulterated profit. We have laughed at the absurdity of the hat economy and shuddered at the manipulative power of the gacha. 

What, then, is the ultimate takeaway from this exploration? Is it that game developers are evil geniuses, twirling their virtual mustaches as they plot new ways to fleece their unsuspecting players? Or is it that we, the players, are simply irrational creatures, slaves to our own primal urges? The truth, as is often the case, lies somewhere in between. The world of game monetization is not a simple morality play of good versus evil, but a complex dance of supply and demand, of art and commerce, of psychology and technology. And as we move forward into an increasingly digital future, it is a dance that we would all do well to learn the steps to. After all, that next virtual unicorn mount is just a click away.

## REFERENCES
